content.text: A capability heat map cross-references GenAI capabilities against business units to reveal where concentration of impact is highest. Without this view, portfolio construction is driven by whoever lobbies loudest — typically the BU with the most politically influential sponsor or the technology team with a favourite solution looking for a problem. The heat map makes concentration visible and defensible, enabling the CoE to direct investment toward the cells where organisational readiness and AI suitability intersect.

attrs.alt: GenAI capability heat map: business units vs AI capabilities

content.text: The Two Axes of the Heat Map
The heat map matrix has two axes:
Y-axis: GenAI capabilities

— the distinct things AI can do: language generation, summarisation, document Q&A, code generation, structured data extraction, classification, vision analysis, predictive insight. Each is a distinct capability with different data requirements, risk profiles, and deployment patterns.
X-axis: Business units
— the organisational entities that own value streams. Typically Sales, Marketing, Operations, Finance, HR, Legal, Product, and Technology. Regulated industries may add Risk, Compliance, and Data.

Each cell is scored High, Medium, Low, or N/A based on two factors: the availability of suitable data within the BU, and the presence of high-impact use cases identified in the value stream scan. N/A indicates either no relevant workflows in the BU or a regulatory constraint that makes the capability inapplicable.

A heat map is not a plan — it is a prioritisation input. High scores indicate where the portfolio should look first, not what it will build. Each High cell still requires a use-case discovery and business case before it enters the portfolio.
The Eight Core GenAI Capabilities
Capability
What It Does
Data Requirement
Primary Risk
Language Generation
Produces coherent text from a prompt or structured input
Domain examples for few-shot or fine-tuning
Hallucination; brand/tone inconsistency
Summarisation
Condenses long content into structured summaries
Source documents; desired output length/format
Omission of key details; compression bias
Document Q&A
Answers questions over a corpus of documents using retrieval-augmented generation
Indexed document corpus; embedding model
Out-of-context retrieval; stale index
Code Generation
Produces functional code from natural language specifications
Domain-specific code examples; test harness
Insecure code; dependency hallucination
Structured Extraction
Parses unstructured text into structured fields
Labelled extraction examples; output schema
Field mapping errors; ambiguous values
Classification
Assigns inputs to predefined categories
Labelled training set per category
Distributional drift; label imbalance
Vision Analysis
Analyses images, PDFs, or video frames for content or anomalies
Labelled visual examples or zero-shot prompts
False positives on edge-case visuals
Predictive Insight
Forecasts outcomes or scores risk from historical patterns
Time-series or tabular historical data
Feature drift; extrapolation beyond training range
Scoring Each Cell
Cells are scored in a structured workshop using three criteria:
Criterion
Question
High Score Signal
Low Score Signal
Data availability
Does the BU have the data this capability needs, in usable form?
Structured data exists, is accessible, and has quality controls
Data is siloed, unstructured, or absent
Use case density
How many distinct high-value use cases exist in the BU for this capability?
3+ use cases with identified owners and baseline metrics
No clear use case or single low-value application
Organisational readiness
Does the BU have the technical literacy and change appetite to adopt AI in this area?
BU has AI guild lead, prior automation experience, and receptive BU GM
No technical contact in BU; prior digital initiatives failed; BU GM sceptical

Each criterion is scored 1–3 and the cell score is the average, rounded to the nearest integer and mapped to High (3), Medium (2), or Low (1). A score of 0 on any single criterion triggers an automatic N/A — insufficient data or zero use cases disqualify the cell regardless of the other scores.
Building and Using the Heat Map: Code Model

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

Score = Literal["High", "Medium", "Low", "NA"]

SCORE_WEIGHT = {"High": 3, "Medium": 2, "Low": 1, "NA": 0}

@dataclass
class HeatCell:
    bu: str
    capability: str
    score: Score
    rationale: str = ""
    use_case_example: str = ""

@dataclass
class CapabilityHeatMap:
    cells: list[HeatCell] = field(default_factory=list)

    def bu_score(self, bu: str) -> float:
        relevant = [c for c in self.cells if c.bu == bu and c.score != "NA"]
        if not relevant:
            return 0.0
        return sum(SCORE_WEIGHT[c.score] for c in relevant) / len(relevant)

    def capability_score(self, capability: str) -> float:
        relevant = [c for c in self.cells if c.capability == capability and c.score != "NA"]
        if not relevant:
            return 0.0
        return sum(SCORE_WEIGHT[c.score] for c in relevant) / len(relevant)

    def top_cells(self, n: int = 5) -> list[HeatCell]:
        scored = [(SCORE_WEIGHT[c.score], c) for c in self.cells if c.score != "NA"]
        return [c for _, c in sorted(scored, key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)[:n]]

    def hot_spots(self) -> list[tuple[str, str, Score]]:
        return [(c.bu, c.capability, c.score) for c in self.cells if c.score == "High"]

# Example usage
heatmap = CapabilityHeatMap(cells=[
    HeatCell("Sales", "Language Generation", "High",
             "Outbound email and proposal drafting",
             "Generate personalised outreach from CRM data"),
    HeatCell("Sales", "Structured Extraction", "High",
             "Extract structured data from unstructured call transcripts",
             "Auto-populate CRM fields post-call"),
    HeatCell("Operations", "Summarisation", "High",
             "High-volume report and log summarisation",
             "Shift handover briefings generated automatically"),
    HeatCell("Finance", "Document Q&A", "High",
             "Policy and contract Q&A against large document sets",
             "Auditor queries against 10-K and purchase agreements"),
    HeatCell("HR", "Language Generation", "High",
             "Job description and offer letter generation",
             "Personalised offer letters from compensation data"),
    HeatCell("Legal", "Structured Extraction", "High",
             "Contract obligation and deadline extraction",
             "Auto-populate obligation tracker from signed contracts"),
    HeatCell("Marketing", "Code Generation", "Low",
             "Marketing team has no coding workflows",
             "N/A"),
])

print("BU scores:")
for bu in ["Sales", "Operations", "Finance", "HR", "Legal", "Marketing"]:
    score = heatmap.bu_score(bu)
    print(f"  {bu}: {score:.1f}/3.0")

print("\nTop 3 hot spots:")
for bu, cap, score in heatmap.hot_spots()[:3]:
    print(f"  [{score}] {bu} x {cap}")

def portfolio_from_heatmap(
    heatmap: CapabilityHeatMap,
    max_portfolio_size: int = 10,
) -> list[HeatCell]:
    """
    Select the top N cells from the heat map as initial portfolio candidates.
    Priority: High > Medium. Within same score, prefer cells with use case examples.
    """
    eligible = [c for c in heatmap.cells if c.score in ("High", "Medium")]
    scored = sorted(
        eligible,
        key=lambda c: (
            SCORE_WEIGHT[c.score],
            1 if c.use_case_example else 0,
        ),
        reverse=True,
    )
    return scored[:max_portfolio_size]

portfolio = portfolio_from_heatmap(heatmap, max_portfolio_size=5)
print("Initial portfolio candidates:")
for i, cell in enumerate(portfolio, 1):
    print(f"  {i}. [{cell.score}] {cell.bu} x {cell.capability}: {cell.use_case_example}")
Reading the Heat Map: What Patterns Mean
Hot BU: One Row Is Mostly Green

A BU with High scores across most capabilities is an early adopter candidate. It typically has good data hygiene, an engaged BU lead, and staff who are already comfortable with automation. Direct the first cohort of use cases here — success in a receptive BU builds the organisational proof that unlocks investment in more resistant BUs.
Hot Capability: One Column Is Mostly Green

A capability that scores High across most BUs is a platform investment priority. Building a shared capability (e.g., a document Q&A layer backed by a common RAG infrastructure) once and making it available to all BUs is more efficient than building BU-specific solutions. Platform capabilities are CoE investments, not BU-funded projects.
Cold BU: One Row Is Mostly Low/NA

A BU with uniformly low scores should not receive Year 1 portfolio investment. The low scores indicate data or readiness gaps that need to be addressed first. Assign a maturity improvement action to the BU and reassess in the next heat map cycle (typically 12 months).
Diagonal Hot Spots

Isolated High cells that do not cluster into hot rows or columns indicate specific high-value use cases. These are often the quickest wins — a single, well-defined use case with clear ownership and data readiness can move from heat map to production within 90 days. Prioritise them as Tier 1 quick wins in the portfolio.
Heat Map Governance and Refresh Cadence
The heat map is a living document. Two types of change make it stale:
Organisational change
— BU restructuring, a new BU GM, or a significant data platform investment changes the readiness scores. Refresh the affected BU column after any major organisational change.
Capability evolution
— New GenAI capabilities (e.g., real-time voice AI, long-context reasoning, multimodal inputs) may not have existed when the heat map was created. Add new capability rows in the annual refresh.
Event
Action
Owner
New GenAI capability enters the market
Add row; score against all BUs in next quarterly review
CoE Lead + GenAI Architect
BU data platform investment completes
Rescore Data Availability for that BU's cells
CoE Lead + BU Data Lead
BU GM or AI guild lead changes
Rescore Organisational Readiness for that BU's cells
PMO
Annual maturity assessment completes
Full heat map refresh using updated readiness data from assessment
CoE Lead

A heat map that has not been refreshed in 18 months is misleading. Stale maps under-score BUs that have invested in data capability and over-score BUs whose readiness has degraded. Make the refresh cadence explicit and calendar it.
Cross-BU Pattern Analysis
The heat map enables three cross-BU analytical patterns that are invisible when BUs are assessed in isolation:
Capability Consolidation Opportunities

When the same capability scores High in multiple BUs, building it as a shared platform service is almost always more efficient than each BU building its own. The consolidation threshold is: if a capability scores High in 3 or more BUs, it belongs in the CoE platform layer, not in BU-specific implementations.
Capability
BUs with High Score
Recommended Approach
Summarisation
Operations, Finance, Legal, HR
Shared summarisation service with BU-specific prompt templates and output formats
Structured Extraction
Sales, Operations, Finance, Legal
Common extraction pipeline with configurable output schemas per BU
Language Generation
Sales, Marketing, HR, Legal
Shared language model access with BU-level system prompt governance
Document Q&A
Finance, HR, Legal
Enterprise RAG platform with BU-segregated indices and access controls

Consolidation reduces total cost of ownership (one platform team, not six), enforces consistent risk controls, and prevents data governance fragmentation. The trade-off is reduced BU autonomy — document it explicitly and resolve in the platform governance charter.
Readiness Sequencing Across BUs
When budget constrains simultaneous BU engagement, the heat map's organisational readiness scores determine sequencing. A practical sequencing heuristic:
Engage BUs with High organisational readiness first (Weeks 1–12 of the programme). These are fast movers who will deliver early proof points.
Run capability discovery in Medium-readiness BUs in parallel but expect 2× longer onboarding timelines.
Defer Low-readiness BUs to Year 2. Assign a dedicated readiness improvement action (guild lead appointment, basic AI literacy training) as a Year 1 pre-condition.

This sequencing avoids the common mistake of starting with the BU that requested AI most loudly — loudness and readiness are not correlated. A loud but Low-readiness BU will absorb disproportionate CoE time with low return.
Risk Profile Concentration

High-scoring cells in high-risk capabilities (Code Generation, Vision Analysis, Predictive Insight) concentrated in a single BU signal a risk concentration that the CISO and risk function should review before portfolio construction begins. Three consecutive High cells in Code Generation across a single BU that handles financial transactions, for example, creates a risk exposure that must be mitigated at the platform level (code review gates, output validation) before any use cases go live.
Workshop Facilitation: Building the Heat Map
The heat map is populated in a structured workshop, not through a questionnaire. Questionnaires produce aspirational scores; workshops produce calibrated scores.
Session
Duration
Participants
Output
Capability briefing
60 min
All BU AI leads + CoE GenAI architect
Shared understanding of what each capability does and doesn't do
BU self-assessment
90 min
Each BU scores its own cells; CoE Lead facilitates
Preliminary scores with one-line rationale per High/Medium cell
Cross-BU calibration
90 min
All BU leads + CoE Lead
Resolved scores where self-assessments conflicted; consolidated heat map
Hot spot prioritisation
60 min
CoE Lead + ESC sponsor
Top 10 hot spots ranked for use case discovery

The cross-BU calibration session is the most important — and the most frequently skipped. Without it, each BU's scores are uncalibrated relative to each other. A Finance BU that rates Document Q&A as High based on a narrow definition of 'high' will appear to have the same opportunity density as an Operations BU that uses a broader definition. The calibration session forces shared definitions.
Calibration Questions
For each disputed or outlier cell, the facilitator asks:
'Show me the data'
— Can the BU demonstrate that the data required for this capability exists, is accessible, and has quality controls? If not, the Data Availability criterion scores 1.
'Name a specific use case'
— Can the BU name a specific workflow, with an owner, where this capability would produce a measurable outcome? If not, Use Case Density scores 1.
'Who would implement it?'
— Does the BU have a named person who would drive implementation? 'The IT team' is not a sufficient answer. Organisational Readiness scores 1 if no named owner exists.
A BU that cannot answer all three questions for a High cell should have the cell reclassified to Medium or Low during calibration. The heat map's value comes from its accuracy, not its optimism.
Heat Map as a Vendor and Platform Decision Input
The heat map informs vendor and platform selection decisions that the CoE must make before or alongside use case delivery:
Heat Map Signal
Vendor/Platform Decision
Language Generation scores High across 4+ BUs
Enterprise LLM API contract (volume pricing, data residency, audit logging) is justified
Document Q&A scores High across 3+ BUs
Invest in a shared RAG platform (vector database, embedding pipeline, retrieval API)
Structured Extraction scores High across 4+ BUs
Standardise on a shared extraction framework; resist BU-specific prompt engineering silos
Vision Analysis scores High only in Operations
BU-funded point solution is appropriate; no shared platform investment justified
Code Generation scores High only in Engineering
Developer tooling (Copilot, Cursor, etc.) is an Engineering decision; CoE governs risk controls only

Platform investments justified by multiple High cells should be owned by the CoE and funded from the central AI budget. Point solutions serving a single BU should be BU-funded with CoE oversight on risk and standards. The heat map makes this distinction explicit and auditable.
Heat Map vs. Use Case Registry

The heat map operates at the intersection level (BU × capability). It answers: 'Where is the opportunity?' The use case registry operates at the specific-use-case level. It answers: 'What exactly will we build?' The heat map feeds the use case registry — each High cell spawns use case discovery that populates the registry with specific, scoped initiatives. The two artefacts are complementary, not competing.
Artefact
Granularity
Updated By
Used For
Capability heat map
BU × GenAI capability type
CoE Lead (annual or on trigger)
Investment strategy; platform prioritisation; BU engagement sequencing
Use case registry
Specific use case with scope, data, owner, and business case
PMO + BU AI leads (continuously)
Portfolio management; resource allocation; sprint planning
Summary
The capability heat map is a BU-by-capability matrix scored High/Medium/Low/NA on three criteria: data availability, use case density, and organisational readiness.
Eight core GenAI capabilities form the rows: Language Generation, Summarisation, Document Q&A, Code Generation, Structured Extraction, Classification, Vision Analysis, and Predictive Insight.
Hot BUs (row mostly High) are early adopter candidates; hot capabilities (column mostly High) are platform investment priorities; isolated High cells are quick-win opportunities.
The heat map is refreshed annually and on trigger events (new AI capability, BU data investment, leadership change). A stale heat map produces misallocated investment.
The heat map feeds the use case registry — each High cell spawns use case discovery that produces the specific, scoped initiatives managed in the portfolio.
